theme: Five Mistakes That Sink Incentive Trips

linkedin.text:
Most incentive trips don't fail on stage. They fail in the spreadsheet weeks before anyone boards a plane.

After 200+ events across 120+ countries, the same 5 mistakes quietly sink "reward" trips:

1) Picking the destination before checking flight access. Two connections and a 6am layover, and the reward already feels like a punishment.
2) Underbudgeting ground transfers. The glamour is in the venue, but trips break in the logistics nobody costed.
3) Over-packing the agenda until the incentive reads like an offsite. Whitespace is the perk.
4) Leaving dietary, accessibility and visa needs to the last week instead of day one.
5) No single on-ground producer when something goes sideways. And on a live program, something always does.

Fix these five and you've handled most of what separates a trip people tolerate from one they talk about for years. That's the whole business: from business to pleasure.

Planning one for 2026? Send us a message for a free second set of eyes on your plan.

#IncentiveTravel #CorporateEvents #EventProduction

instagram.text:
5 mistakes that quietly sink incentive trips 🧩

The reward almost never fails on the trip.
It fails in the planning.

After 200+ events, here's what wrecks them 👇

✈️ Picking the destination before checking flight access
🚐 Underbudgeting transfers (logistics is where trips break)
🗓️ Cramming the agenda till "reward" feels like work
🥗 Leaving dietary / accessibility / visa needs too late
🎧 No single on-ground producer when it goes sideways — and it does

Save this before you plan your next one.
Tag the person who books your company trips 👇

From business to pleasure — that's the whole point.

facebook.text:
Incentive trips rarely fail on the trip — they fail in the planning. 🧩

After 200+ events, here are the 5 mistakes we see sink them: booking the destination before checking flight access, underbudgeting transfers, cramming the agenda until "reward" feels like work, leaving dietary/accessibility/visa needs too late, and having no single on-ground producer when something goes sideways.

Get these right and you're most of the way to a trip people actually remember — from business to pleasure.
